content.assessment: From a network-planning perspective, status quo maintenance is geographically feasible by definition since it requires no new infrastructure, but the underlying network is already deteriorating: over 1,200 of 3,155 settlements lack any public educational institution, primary feeder sites shrank from 3,609 to 3,521 (2021-2024), and rural teacher shortages/aging workforce constrain capacity. Monitoring alone does nothing to arrest network polarization by settlement type or the geographic concentration of elite early-selective tracks in Budapest and a few centers.
opportunities: Monitoring infrastructure could be used to map network gaps (the 1,200+ settlements without schools) ahead of future reform
strengths: Requires no new capacity, transport, or facility investment since no structural change occurs
Monitoring could generate the geographic/capacity data currently missing to verify the 'under one hour' access claim for 6-/8-year tracks
threats: Continued settlement-level polarization and rural teacher shortages will likely widen geographic access gaps regardless of monitoring
Rising urban/suburban child-teacher ratios (up to 24:1) may worsen capacity strain absent intervention
weaknesses: Does not address the documented shrinkage of the primary feeder network (3,609 to 3,521 sites) from which selection candidates are drawn
Fails to address rural teacher shortages and aging workforce that already constrain capacity outside major cities
Leaves unresolved the geographic concentration of top-tier 6-/8-year tracks in Budapest and a few regional centers
